# Ten advanced quant research foundations for ADX Quant Pro

These are research/shadow-layer recommendations. None should replace Field 1, thresholds, Power BI, regime logic, TP/SL, or production weights until completed-H1 walk-forward evidence passes promotion gates.

## 1. Hamilton (1989) — “A New Approach to the Economic Analysis of Nonstationary Time Series and the Business Cycle”
**Concept and theorem:** A time series may be generated by an unobserved finite-state Markov chain. Conditional distributions and transition probabilities infer the probability of each regime.
**System use:** Run a shadow probability vector for trend, compression, range, and expansion. Compare it with the protected regime rather than replacing it. Use disagreement as transition-risk evidence and lower actionability when state probability is diffuse.
**Benefit:** Fewer abrupt regime mistakes and a measurable lifecycle probability instead of a single hard label.

## 2. Pesaran & Timmermann (1992) — “A Simple Nonparametric Test of Predictive Performance”
**Concept and theorem:** Tests whether predicted and realized directions are associated beyond what unconditional BUY/SELL frequencies imply.
**System use:** Apply to settled H1 direction confirmation globally and separately by London, Asian, and overlap sessions. Require sufficient sample size and report p-values only as evidence.
**Benefit:** Separates genuine directional skill from a model that merely predicts the majority direction.

## 3. Diebold & Mariano (1995) — “Comparing Predictive Accuracy”
**Concept and theorem:** Uses the mean and long-run variance of loss differentials to test whether two forecasts have equal predictive accuracy.
**System use:** Compare the protected Power BI path with each shadow model at H+1, H+3, and H+6 using absolute error, squared error, and directional loss. Use Newey–West/HAC variance for overlapping horizons.
**Benefit:** Prevents replacing the current path based on visually attractive but statistically weak backtests.

## 4. Christoffersen (1998) — “Evaluating Interval Forecasts”
**Concept and theorem:** Valid prediction intervals need correct unconditional coverage and independent violations; the conditional-coverage test combines both.
**System use:** Audit Field 2 upper/lower bands by horizon and session. Flag clustered misses, not only average coverage. Adapt only a shadow interval width until validated.
**Benefit:** More honest uncertainty bands and better HOLD/NO-TRADE decisions when interval reliability deteriorates.

## 5. Gneiting, Balabdaoui & Raftery (2007) — “Probabilistic Forecasts, Calibration and Sharpness”
**Concept and belief:** A good probabilistic forecast is calibrated and, subject to calibration, as sharp as possible. Proper scoring rules reward honest distributions.
**System use:** Track Brier score, log score, CRPS, calibration slope/intercept, and interval width. Confidence may be promoted only when calibration improves without uselessly wide bands.
**Benefit:** Reliability percentages become empirically meaningful rather than cosmetic confidence numbers.

## 6. Bifet & Gavaldà (2007) — “Learning from Time-Changing Data with Adaptive Windowing” (ADWIN)
**Concept and theorem:** Maintains a variable-length window and signals drift when two subwindows have statistically different means under a controlled false-alarm bound.
**System use:** Monitor settled direction errors, absolute forecast residuals, coverage misses, and decision conflicts. On drift, reduce confidence, freeze promotion, or shorten a research window; never silently rewrite production logic.
**Benefit:** Prevents stale evidence from dominating after market behavior changes.

## 7. Romano, Patterson & Candès (2019) — “Conformalized Quantile Regression”
**Concept and theorem:** Combines quantile regression with conformal calibration to create heteroscedastic prediction intervals with finite-sample marginal coverage under exchangeability.
**System use:** Build research-only session-conditional H+1/H+3/H+6 intervals from completed-candle residuals. For temporal dependence, use blocked calibration and compare with adaptive conformal methods.
**Benefit:** Bands widen in difficult periods and narrow in stable periods while preserving a measurable coverage target.

## 8. Gibbs & Candès (2021) — “Adaptive Conformal Inference Under Distribution Shift”
**Concept and theorem:** Updates the conformal miscoverage level online so long-run coverage can be controlled when the data distribution changes.
**System use:** Feed only settled residuals into an adaptive coverage controller. Cap step size, maintain horizon/session separation, and log every width adjustment.
**Benefit:** Reduces repeated “coverage insufficient” states and makes uncertainty responsive to regime shifts without changing the central prediction.

## 9. Hansen (2005) — “A Test for Superior Predictive Ability”
**Concept and theorem:** Tests whether a benchmark is outperformed by any candidate while controlling data-snooping effects across many alternatives.
**System use:** Evaluate threshold variants, model variants, and research factors against the protected system after costs. Candidates failing SPA remain shadow-only.
**Benefit:** Greatly lowers false discoveries from testing many “improvements.”

## 10. Hansen, Lunde & Nason (2011) — “The Model Confidence Set”
**Concept and theorem:** Sequentially removes inferior models until a statistically defensible set of superior models remains at a chosen confidence level.
**System use:** Maintain a set of non-inferior shadow models by horizon, regime, and session. Use their disagreement as uncertainty; do not automatically average them into production.
**Benefit:** Avoids unstable winner-take-all selection and gives a robust research leaderboard.

# Quant-level promotion protocol

1. Use completed broker H1 candles only and immutable run identities.
2. Use anchored walk-forward folds with purging/embargo for overlapping H+1/H+3/H+6 labels.
3. Keep training, calibration, and final test windows separate.
4. Include spread, slippage, turnover, and abstention costs.
5. Report session/regime slices plus global results; require a minimum settled sample in every promoted slice.
6. Calibrate probabilities out of sample with Platt scaling or isotonic regression selected only on calibration data.
7. Require positive expected value, non-inferior drawdown, stable coverage, lower or equal error, and no material turnover explosion.
8. Require Pesaran–Timmermann directional evidence, Diebold–Mariano forecast evidence, Christoffersen coverage evidence, SPA survival, and Model Confidence Set membership.
9. Detect drift before promotion and after deployment. A drift alarm freezes promotion and lowers research confidence.
10. Promotion must be explicit, versioned, reversible, and never overwrite historical production decisions.
